lesson.menu_desc: Discourse markers for structured opinions -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- Connecting Ideas -- Discourse Markers
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari | Aula 7 -- Connecting Ideas

listenings.text: Good morning, students. This is your school radio editorial. Today I want to give you my honest opinion about a popular idea: should our school start one hour later? In my view, the answer is yes. First of all, many teenagers go to bed late and wake up tired. Therefore, a later start could help us focus better in the morning. Moreover, several studies show that students who sleep more get higher grades. For instance, one school in another city tried a later start and saw much better results. However, I understand the other side. Some parents work early and cannot drive their children later. In addition, sports practice might finish after dark. Nevertheless, I believe the benefits are stronger than the problems. In conclusion, a later start would make us healthier and more focused. Thank you for listening, and have a great day.

Hi everyone, and welcome back to the Teen Voices podcast. In today's short episode, I want to share my opinion on a simple question: are team sports better than individual sports for teenagers? Personally, I think both are valuable, but team sports have a special advantage. To begin with, they teach you to work with other people and to trust your teammates. In addition, they make exercise feel fun rather than boring. For instance, a tough training session feels much easier when your friends are next to you. On the other hand, individual sports like running or swimming give you more freedom and a flexible schedule. Some people love that independence, whereas others feel lonely without a team. Despite these differences, the most important thing is simply to stay active. As a result, my advice is easy: choose the sport you enjoy, and keep moving. That is all for today. See you next week.

extra_audio.text: First, you state your opinion clearly. Then you give your first reason. Next, you add a second reason with moreover or in addition. After that, you mention the other side with however or on the other hand. Finally, you sum up your view with in conclusion.
